redFlags.whyItMatters: The solicitation's Evaluation Criteria C (Exhibit A, Section C.3.6) allocates 35 out of 100 base points to 'Contractor's Capability to provide the services and expertise and Past Performance,' making it the single highest-weighted evaluation category. This section requires relevant experience with governmental agencies, specific experience with Federal reporting, three examples of successfully delivered SaaS/IT projects, a list of government contracts performed within the past five years, and reference checks. Exhibit B, Section D.1(d)(1) further mandates proof of prior work with at least 10 public agencies including 2 municipalities of similar size to San Diego. A search of HORUSTECH's past performance records returned no relevant projects in grants management, municipal SaaS, or comparable government technology implementations. Without a strong past performance portfolio in this domain, HORUSTECH faces a critical scoring disadvantage on 35% of the base evaluation points.
redFlags.mitigation: The capture manager should conduct an immediate go/no-go assessment focused on whether HORUSTECH can credibly compete on the 35-point past performance category, either through direct experience or through a teaming arrangement with an established grants management SaaS provider. If teaming is pursued, the business development lead must identify and engage a partner with verifiable government grants management deployments (including HUD programs) no later than one week before the March 16 deadline. If neither direct experience nor a viable teaming partner can be identified, the leadership team should seriously consider a no-bid decision to avoid investing resources in a low-probability-of-win pursuit.
redFlags.flag: No past performance found for HORUSTECH in grants management or comparable municipal SaaS implementations.

redFlags.whyItMatters: Exhibit B, Section 3(d) states 'Due to sunsetting of the existing system, the desired go-live date for a new solution is July 1, 2026.' The current grant management operations use Salesforce-backed reporting (Exhibit B, Section A.2) and cloud-based Microsoft programs with SeamlessDocs (Exhibit B, Section A.1(a)). While no incumbent contractor is explicitly named, the sunsetting language suggests the City has an urgent operational need and may favor proposers who can demonstrate rapid deployment capability and familiarity with the City's existing data structures. Proposers who have previously worked with the City's Economic Development Department or who offer solutions already deployed at comparable HUD-funded programs may have an inherent advantage in demonstrating readiness to meet the aggressive July 1 timeline. This urgency-driven procurement environment disadvantages new entrants who would need more time for discovery and configuration.
redFlags.mitigation: The proposal team should emphasize any rapid deployment capabilities, pre-built HUD compliance modules, or accelerated implementation methodologies in the proposal narrative. The capture lead should also submit a question via PlanetBids before February 23 asking whether the July 1, 2026 go-live date is firm or if the City would consider a phased approach, which would help level the playing field for proposers who are not already familiar with the City's existing systems and data.
redFlags.flag: Existing system sunsetting creates urgency that may favor an incumbent or known solution provider.

risks.whyItMatters: Per Exhibit B, Section 3(d) - Project Schedule, the City states 'the desired go-live date for a new solution is July 1, 2026.' Given that proposals are due March 16, 2026, and contract execution will follow evaluation, oral presentations, and administrative processing, the Contractor would likely have only 2-3 months to complete configuration, data migration from multiple existing systems (SharePoint, SeamlessDocs, Salesforce per Section 5(e)), training for up to 285 users (75 internal, 10 admin, 200 external per Section 3(e)), and full testing across three environments (development, testing, production per Section 3(c)). This compressed timeline significantly increases the risk of incomplete implementation, inadequate testing, or failure to meet acceptance criteria, which could damage the proposer's past performance record and trigger default provisions under Article IV of the General Contract Terms.
risks.mitigation: The capture team lead should immediately develop a detailed implementation timeline demonstrating feasibility of the July 1, 2026 go-live, or propose a phased go-live approach with the Business Districts program launching first and Community Development Programs following. The project manager should prepare a risk-adjusted schedule with clear milestones and present it in the proposal, and the proposal team should submit a question via PlanetBids before February 23, 2026 asking whether the City would accept a phased deployment approach if it ensures higher quality delivery.
risks.flag: Aggressive go-live timeline of July 1, 2026 for a complex SaaS implementation.

risks.whyItMatters: Exhibit B, Section D.1(d)(1) explicitly requires that 'The Proposer shall demonstrate capability by providing proof of prior similar work with at least 10 cities, states, or other public agencies. At least 2 examples shall be used by a municipality of similar size and complexity.' San Diego is the 8th largest city in the United States, meaning the proposer must show deployments at comparably large municipalities. This is a significant barrier to entry that could disqualify proposers who primarily serve smaller jurisdictions or private sector clients. Additionally, Section D.1(d)(2) requires sample cases demonstrating functionality across program and financial reporting needs, further raising the evidence threshold. Failure to meet this requirement would likely render the proposal non-responsive, as it is framed as a mandatory demonstration of capability rather than a scored preference.
risks.mitigation: The business development lead should immediately audit HORUSTECH's client portfolio to identify at least 10 qualifying public agency deployments and 2 large-municipality references of similar size to San Diego (population 1.3M+). If the company cannot meet this threshold independently, the capture manager should explore teaming arrangements or subcontracting with an established grants management SaaS provider who has the required public sector track record, and formalize any such arrangement before the March 16 proposal deadline.
risks.flag: Mandatory demonstration of prior work with at least 10 public agencies, including 2 municipalities of similar size and complexity.

risks.whyItMatters: Exhibit B, Section D.1(c) requires 'Understanding of HUD regulations and requirements to ensure that Federal requirements are built into the SaaS solution and not customized.' Additionally, Evaluation Criteria B.4 (Exhibit A, Section C.3.6) scores proposers on 'Description of the company and/or key managers' experience and knowledge of local and federal regulations related to CDBG, ESG, and HOME,' and Criteria C.3 evaluates 'Specific experience with Federal reporting and ability to integrate to Federal reporting systems.' The solution must also integrate with HUD's IDIS system per Exhibit B, Section 5(e)(1). This domain-specific expertise in federal grant compliance is a specialized niche, and proposers without demonstrated HUD program experience will score poorly across multiple evaluation categories totaling up to 55 points (Sections A, B, and C combined). Without this expertise, the proposal will be fundamentally uncompetitive.
risks.mitigation: The proposal manager should identify and recruit subject matter experts with direct HUD CDBG/ESG/HOME program experience to serve as key personnel or advisors on the project team. Resumes demonstrating federal grant management expertise should be included per Exhibit B, Section D.2(e). If HORUSTECH lacks in-house HUD expertise, the capture lead should pursue a subcontracting partnership with a firm specializing in HUD compliance software by no later than one week before the March 16 deadline to allow time for integration into the proposal narrative.
risks.flag: Requirement for HUD/CDBG/ESG/HOME regulatory expertise built into the SaaS solution.

risks.whyItMatters: Per Exhibit A, Section C.3.3, proposers scoring within 10 points of the highest-scoring proposal will be required to interview and/or make an oral presentation, and 'Proposers are required to complete their oral presentation and/or interviews within seven (7) workdays after the City's request.' The oral presentation carries 15 out of 100 base points and includes a live software demonstration ('Real Time Operation including demonstration of the software meeting key requirements' and 'Usability of Software'). This compressed 7-workday window requires the proposer to have a fully functional demo environment prepared in advance, along with key personnel available on short notice. Multiple interviews may be required, and key personnel may need to interview individually. Failure to comply within the timeframe could result in disqualification from the final evaluation round.
risks.mitigation: The project manager should begin preparing a demo environment and presentation materials in parallel with proposal development, ensuring the software can demonstrate key grant lifecycle workflows (application intake, scoring, reimbursement processing, reporting). The capture lead should confirm availability of all key personnel named in the proposal for the anticipated oral presentation window (likely April-May 2026) and establish a standby protocol so the team can mobilize within 2-3 business days of receiving the City's request.
risks.flag: Mandatory oral presentation/interview within 7 workdays of City's request for top-scoring proposers.

risks.whyItMatters: Per Exhibit A, Section C.3.6(F), the City awards up to 12 additional percentage points to the proposer's final score for Small Local Business Enterprise (SLBE) or Emerging Local Business Enterprise (ELBE) participation. With a base maximum of 100 points, these 12 bonus points represent a significant scoring advantage — effectively increasing the maximum possible score to 112 points. A proposer without SLBE/ELBE participation could be at a 12-point disadvantage compared to a competitor who maximizes this category. Given that the price category is only worth 10 points, the SLBE/ELBE bonus alone exceeds the entire price evaluation weight, meaning local business participation is more impactful than having the lowest price. For firms without an existing San Diego presence or local subcontractor relationships, this creates a meaningful competitive gap.
risks.mitigation: The business development lead should immediately research and identify qualified SLBE or ELBE firms in the San Diego area that could serve as subcontractors for training, implementation support, change management, or local help desk services. The capture manager should reach out to potential SLBE/ELBE partners before the February 23 questions deadline and formalize teaming agreements with committed participation percentages before the March 16 proposal submission to maximize the bonus points available under the Equal Opportunity Contracting provisions.
risks.flag: SLBE/ELBE participation provides up to 12 bonus points, creating a competitive disadvantage for firms without local San Diego partnerships.

risks.whyItMatters: Exhibit B, Section 5(e)(2-3) states that 'Sources of data that need to be migrated into the selected system include, but are not limited to, individual project files hosted in Microsoft SharePoint, Seamless Docs and Salesforce backed programs,' and that 'Proposals shall include the necessary process for migrating the grant data and files from the existing systems. This includes indexing and integrating existing content into the new system.' The solicitation provides limited detail on the volume, format, or quality of existing data across these three disparate platforms. Data migration from multiple legacy systems is inherently risky and time-consuming, and the lack of specificity about data volumes and structures makes it difficult to accurately scope and price this effort. Underestimating migration complexity could lead to cost overruns on a firm-fixed-price contract per Exhibit A, Section B.1.
risks.mitigation: The proposal team should submit a question via PlanetBids before February 23, 2026 requesting details on the approximate volume of records, documents, and data to be migrated from each source system (SharePoint, SeamlessDocs, Salesforce). The technical lead should include a detailed data migration methodology in the proposal with clear assumptions about data volumes and quality, and build contingency into the pricing model to account for unknown data complexity while remaining competitive on the 10-point price evaluation.
risks.flag: Complex data migration from multiple existing systems (SharePoint, SeamlessDocs, Salesforce) with limited documentation.

risks.whyItMatters: Exhibit B, Section G requires Cyber Liability insurance with limits of not less than $1,000,000 per occurrence and $2,000,000 aggregate, plus Professional Liability coverage with limits of at least $1,000,000 per occurrence and $2,000,000 aggregate. These are in addition to the standard Commercial General Liability, Commercial Automobile Liability, and Workers' Compensation requirements in Article VII of the General Contract Terms. The Cyber Liability policy must specifically cover privacy violations, information theft, damage to electronic information, intentional/unintentional release of private information, extortion, and network security. For smaller technology firms, obtaining or increasing cyber liability coverage to these levels can be costly and time-consuming, and evidence of all required insurance must be submitted within 10 business days of the Notice of Intent to Award per Exhibit A, Section F.1.
risks.mitigation: The contracts or finance lead should immediately contact HORUSTECH's insurance broker to verify current coverage levels for Cyber Liability and Professional Liability, and obtain quotes for any necessary increases to meet the $1M/$2M thresholds specified in Exhibit B, Section G. This should be completed before proposal submission so that accurate insurance costs can be factored into the price proposal, and the broker should be placed on standby to issue certificates and endorsements within the 10-business-day post-award window.
risks.flag: Multiple specialized insurance requirements including Cyber Liability and Professional Liability coverage.

risks.whyItMatters: Exhibit B, Section 4(b) requires the Contractor to warrant 99.9% Uptime Availability each calendar month, with service credits ranging from 10% to 25% of the prorated monthly subscription fee for failures to meet this threshold. While 99.9% uptime is a common SaaS industry standard, the tiered penalty structure (10% credit for 99.7-99.9%, 15% for 99.5-99.7%, and 25% for below 99.5%) creates financial exposure if the platform experiences unexpected outages. The calculation formula in Section 4(b)(2) excludes scheduled maintenance and force majeure events, which provides some protection, but proposers must ensure their hosting infrastructure can consistently meet this standard across the full 5-year contract term.
risks.mitigation: The technical lead should document the proposed hosting infrastructure's historical uptime performance and include this data in the proposal to demonstrate capability. The proposal team should also review the service credit structure and ensure the pricing model accounts for potential credit obligations, and the system architect should confirm that the proposed architecture includes redundancy and failover mechanisms sufficient to maintain 99.9% availability.
risks.flag: Uptime SLA of 99.9% with tiered service credit penalties for non-compliance.

incumbentInfo.notes: The solicitation does not name a current contract holder. The City currently uses Salesforce-backed reporting for Community Development Programs and cloud-based Microsoft programs (Excel, Word, SharePoint) with SeamlessDocs for Business Districts (Exhibit B, Section A). These are product/platform names, not incumbent contractors. The solicitation mentions 'sunsetting of the existing system' (Exhibit B, Section 3(d)) but does not use terms like 'recompete', 'follow-on', or 'predecessor contract.' This appears to be a new procurement for a consolidated grants management SaaS solution rather than a recompete of an existing contract.
